1.5: The evening primrose . This animal is very famous in the East because of its speed, and it seems that this quality is the source of its oriental name. It is said that no rider can catch it. Oriental poets compare a squadron of horsemen passing by with lightning speed to a herd of onagers. It is a very wild animal, ash-red in color, with long ears, and lives in herds in the deserts.
1.1: . God proclaims his innocence before his friends, and their injustice is forgiven only through his intercession, verses
1.2: . Job himself is rewarded: he will know that a trial well endured becomes a source of happiness; he receives double the possessions he had lost, verses
1.12: Job, praying for his friends according to God's command, humbled himself in His presence, and his humility, combined with the charity that moved him to intercede for those who had wronged him, earned him a great increase in all his possessions as a reward. But, as Saint Augustine observes, ( Epist. (CXX, chap. X), it would have been little for Job to receive temporally twice what he had previously possessed, as a reward for the admirable steadfastness with which he had endured such a terrible trial of his virtue. It is therefore primarily the blessedness of the afterlife that the Holy Spirit wished to represent to us through this prosperity, far greater than the first, with which the Lord rewarded his faithfulness.
1.6: . He knew that God was great and that His ways were incomprehensible, but he didn't fully grasp it; he confesses that he was wrong to presumptuously challenge God and he begs for forgiveness. The discussion thus ends as it should, with God's complete victory, a victory acknowledged and accepted by humankind, which can achieve no other than this: recognizing its nothingness in the presence of its creator.
1.11: Several commentators thought they recognized the elephant in Behemoth. The feature we have here: her virtue in the navel of her belly It is perfectly suited to the hippopotamus, but not to the elephant, whose belly skin is quite tender.
1.15: The hippopotamus has teeth shaped like a curved, harped saber; and the elephant, besides its teeth, has a trunk for a tusk. — All commentators today recognize in this sword the hippopotamus's teeth. "With its teeth," said Wood, "the hippopotamus can bend the grass as evenly as with a scythe." These teeth, said Abbot Prévot, in his History of travel , "They are harder and whiter than ivory." The hippopotamus grazes on the hills bordering the Nile in Upper Egypt. All the field animals play around it. Since it is a herbivore and not a carnivore, the other animals have nothing to fear.
1.16: . — Job's ordeal is now over. He has unknowingly thwarted Satan's plan: —

1.9: They will shout ; That is to say, the wicked oppressed by other wicked people will cry out, but they will not be heard, because they will not cry out to God, as it says in verse
1.13: And their father gave them , etc.; that is to say, Job gave his daughters their share of his inheritance just as he did his sons. The author of the Book of Job, who was Hebrew, makes this observation because in his nation daughters did not inherit when they had brothers (see Numbers
1.26: indicates, it was very difficult to catch. — Merchants , in Hebrew; the Canaanites or Phoenicians who were such famous merchants that their name had become synonymous with merchants.
1.31: Were crocodiles already being harpooned in Job's time? This verse seems to prove the contrary, or at least the ineffectiveness of the method. Moreover, even today, harpooning is generally ineffective unless it strikes the animal precisely between the neck and head or in the belly. The projectiles slide off its scales without damaging them.
1.14: Naturalists and travelers report contradictory things on this point. While the ostrich does not entirely neglect its eggs, it seems certain that it takes little care of them, especially in the days following laying, and that it always abandons them when pursued by hunters.
1.18: She laughed ; etc. Pliny says (Book X, Chapter I) that when the ostrich is pursued by hunters, it spreads its wings, using them like sails to help it run, and that it runs with a speed approaching that of the fastest flight. Diodorus Siculus adds (Book II) that while running, it throws stones behind it with its feet so violently that it often kills the hunters. Naturalists confirm these details. "The ostrich's run is very fast," says one of them. "The most agile greyhounds cannot catch them. Even the Arab, mounted on his horse, is forced to resort to trickery to catch them, skillfully throwing a stick at their legs. In their flight, they throw pebbles behind them like arrows at their pursuers."«
1.23: The sea . The crocodile usually lives in fresh water; but it should not be forgotten that the Nile is called in Scripture sea , because of its size and its regular floods, which last so long, and because in the style of the Hebrews all large bodies of water, lakes, ponds, bear the name of seas .
1.10: Her eyes are like the eyelids of dawn. . The crocodile's eyes are the hieroglyphic sign in Egyptian that represents dawn.
1.27: All its paths . This is the usual translation; in our opinion, it would be more accurate to say: None, none of her ways ; because, as we have already noted, the word All In Hebrew, when joined to a negation, it means none, none .
1.17: When the crocodile appears out of the water, the most powerful and influential men in the country will be seized with fear and terror. They will purify themselves of their sins, by doing penance.
1.22: Nature has provided crocodiles with protection by equipping them with an almost impenetrable armor; their entire body is covered in scales, except for the top of their head, where the skin adheres directly to the bone. These square scales possess great hardness and a flexibility that prevents them from being brittle; the center of these blades features a kind of hard ridge that adds to their strength. Thévenot compares the crocodile's back, because of these points, to a door entirely studded with iron nails and so hard that no spear could pierce it.
1.24: Behind him , etc. The speed and movement of the crocodile in the water are such that it leaves traces of its passage by a long furrow of foam and by the whiteness of the water like the white hair of an old man.
1.29: It was assumed that God lived in a tent or pavilion made of clouds that surrounded him on all sides and hid him from the sight of men.
1.30: Friends , etc. Will your friends carve up Leviathan at your table, or will merchants trade in it? — Or rather, the partners, the fishermen who join together for fishing. — Although the crocodile was sacred in some parts of Egypt, at Elephantine and Apollinopolis, it was salted and sold in pieces, but this could not be done often, because, as verse

The middle is occupied by livestock; the section reserved for the human inhabitants is marked and delimited by a line of stones. And yet the indolent, well-built men who own this stable are too lazy to build themselves a hut. 2.1: It came to pass one day that, when the sons of God came to present themselves before the Lord, Satan also came among them to present himself before the Lord.
2.2: And the Lord said to Satan, «Where do you come from?» Satan answered the Lord and said, «From roaming the world and walking around on it.»
2.3: The Lord said to Satan, «Have you considered my servant Job? There is no one like him on earth, blameless and upright, fearing God and turning away from evil. He always maintains his integrity, even though you have provoked me to destroy him without cause.»
2.4: Satan answered the Lord and said, «Skin for skin. A man gives what he has to preserve his life.
2.5: But stretch out your hand, touch his bones and his flesh, and we'll see if he doesn't curse you to your face.»
2.6: The Lord said to Satan, «Behold, I deliver him into your hands; only spare his life.»
2.7: And Satan went out from the presence of the Lord. And he struck Job with a painful leprosy from the soles of his feet to the crown of his head.
2.8: And Job took a piece of broken pottery to scrape his wounds and he sat down on the ashes.
2.9: And his wife said to him, "You still persist in your integrity. Curse God and die."«
2.10: He said to her, «You speak like a foolish woman. Shall we accept good from God and not also evil?» In all this, Job did not sin with his lips.
2.11: Three friends of Job, Eliphaz the Temanite, Baldad the Shuhite and Zophar the Naamathite, heard of all the misfortunes that had come upon him, they each went from their country and consulted with one another to come and grieve for him and comfort him.
2.12: Having looked up from afar, they did not recognize him, and they raised their voices and wept, they tore each of their cloaks and threw dust toward the sky above their heads.
2.13: And they sat on the ground beside him seven days and seven nights, without any of them saying a word to him, because they saw how great his pain was.

3.1: Then Job opened his mouth and cursed the day he was born.
3.3: May the day I was born perish, and the night that said, "A man is conceived."«
3.4: On that day, let it turn into darkness; let God above not be concerned, let no light shine upon it.
3.5: Let darkness and the shadow of death claim him, let a thick cloud cover him, let the eclipse of his light cast terror.
3.6: Let darkness take hold of this night; let it not be counted among the days of the year, nor entered into the reckoning of the months.
3.7: May this night be a barren desert, may no cry of joy be heard there.
3.8: Let those who curse the days, who know how to evoke Leviathan, curse her.
3.9: May the stars of her twilight grow dark, may she wait for the light, without it coming, and may she not see the eyelids of dawn,
3.10: because she did not close the doors of the breast to me and did not hide suffering from my sight.
3.11: Why did I not die in my mother's womb, and come forth from her loins, why did I not expire?.
3.12: Why did I find two knees to receive me on, and why breasts to suckle?
3.13: Now I would lie down in peace, I would sleep and rest.
3.14: with the kings and great men of the earth, who built themselves mausoleums,
3.15: with the princes who had gold and filled their dwellings with silver.
3.16: Or, like the ignored abortion, I would not exist, like those children who have not seen the light.
3.17: There the wicked no longer wreak their violence, there the man, exhausted of strength, finds rest.,
3.18: The captives are all at peace there; they no longer hear the voice of the work master.
3.19: There are the small and the great, the slave freed from his master.
3.20: Why give light to the unfortunate and life to those whose souls are filled with bitterness?,
3.21: who long for death and death does not come, who seek it more ardently than treasures,
3.22: who are happy, who leap for joy and rejoice when they have found the tomb,
3.23: to the man whose path is hidden and whom God has enclosed on all sides?
3.24: My sighs are like my bread, and my groans flow like water.
3.25: What I fear is what is happening to me; what I dread is bearing down on me.
3.26: No more tranquility, no more peace, no more rest, and turmoil seized me.

4.1: Then Eliphaz the Temanite spoke up and said:
4.2: If we risk a word, perhaps you will be distressed, but who could restrain their words?
4.3: Behold, you have instructed many, you have strengthened their weak hands,
4.4: that your words have lifted up those who were faltering, that you have strengthened the faltering knees
4.5: And now that misfortune comes to you, you weaken; now that it reaches you, you lose courage.
4.6: Was not your fear of God your hope? Was not your trust in the purity of your life?
4.7: Search your memory: who was the innocent person who perished? In what place in the world were the righteous exterminated?
4.8: For me, I have seen it: those who plow iniquity and sow injustice reap its fruits.
4.9: By the breath of God they perish, they are consumed by the wind of his anger.
4.10: The lion's roar and thundering voice are stifled, and the young lion's teeth are broken.,
4.11: The lion perished for lack of prey, and the lioness's cubs scattered.
4.12: A word reached me stealthily and my ear caught its faint murmur.
4.13: In the hazy visions of the night, at the hour when deep sleep weighs upon mortals,
4.14: A fear and trembling seized me and shook all my bones.
4.15: A spirit passed before me. The hairs on my skin stood on end.
4.16: He stood up, I didn't recognize his face, like a ghost before my eyes. A great silence, then I heard a voice:
4.17: Will man be just in the eyes of God? Will a mortal be pure before his Creator?
4.18: Behold, he does not trust his servants and finds fault in his angels:
4.19: How much more so those who live in mud houses, whose foundations are in the dust, who will be reduced to dust, as by a moth.
4.20: From morning till night they are exterminated and without anyone noticing, they perish forever.
4.21: The rope of their tent is cut; they die before they have known wisdom.

5.1: Call out then. Will anyone answer you? To which of the saints will you turn?
5.2: Anger kills the fool, and rage kills the madman.
5.3: I saw the fool spreading his roots and suddenly I cursed his dwelling.
5.4: There is no salvation for his sons, they are crushed at the gate and no one defends them.
5.5: The hungry man devours his harvest, he leaps over the hedge of thorns and carries it off; the thirsty man swallows up his riches.
5.6: For misfortune does not spring from dust, nor does suffering sprout from the ground,
5.7: so that man is born for suffering, like the sons of lightning to raise their flight.
5.8: If I were you, I would turn to God; it is to Him that I would direct my prayer.
5.9: He does great things, which cannot be fathomed, wonders that cannot be counted.
5.10: He pours rain on the earth, he sends waters upon the fields,
5.11: It exalts those who are humbled, and the afflicted find happiness again.
5.12: He thwarts the plans of the treacherous, and their hands cannot carry out their plots.
5.13: He catches the clever in their own cunning and overturns the advice of shrewd men.
5.14: During the day they encounter darkness, at midday they grope as if in the night.
5.15: God saves the weak from the sword of their tongue and from the hand of the powerful.
5.16: Then hope returns to the unfortunate, and iniquity shuts its mouth.
5.17: Blessed is the man whom God chastens. Therefore, do not despise the correction of the Almighty.
5.18: For he wounds and he binds up, he strikes and his hand heals.
5.19: Six times he will deliver you from anguish, and on the seventh, no harm will touch you.
5.20: In famine, he will save you from death; in battle, from the blows of the sword.
5.21: You will be safe from the lash of the tongue, you will be without fear when devastation comes.
5.22: You will laugh at devastation and famine, you will not fear the beasts of the earth
5.23: for you will have a covenant with the stones of the field, and the beasts of the earth will be at peace with you.
5.24: You will see happiness reign in your tent, you will visit your pastures and nothing will be lacking.
5.25: You will see your descendants increase and your offspring multiply like the grass of the field.
5.26: You will enter the tomb ripe, like a sheaf of wheat harvested in its season.
5.27: This is what we have observed: it is the truth. Listen to it and take advantage of it.

6.1: Then Job spoke up and said:
6.2: Oh, if only it were possible to weigh my affliction and put all my calamities together in the balance
6.3: They would be heavier than the sand of the sea: that is why my words border on madness.
6.4: For the arrows of the Almighty pierce me and my soul drinks their venom; the terrors of God are arrayed in battle against me.
6.5: Does the onager roar beside the tender grass? Does the ox low before its pasture?
6.6: How can one nourish oneself with a bland, salt-free dish, or find flavor in the juice of a tasteless herb?
6.7: What my soul refuses to touch, that is my bread, all covered in defilement.
6.8: Who will grant me that my wish may come true and that God may fulfill my expectation?
6.9: May God deign to break me, may he let his hand go and cut off my days
6.10: And may I at least have this consolation, which makes me tremble in the midst of the afflictions with which he overwhelms me: that I have never transgressed the commandments of the Holy One
6.11: What is my strength, that I should wait? How long are my days, that I should be patient?
6.12: Is my strength the strength of stones, and is my flesh bronze?
6.13: Am I not without all help and has all hope of salvation been taken from me?
6.14: The unfortunate man has a right to the pity of his friend, even if he had abandoned the fear of the Almighty.
6.15: My brothers have been treacherous like the torrent, like the water of the flowing torrents.
6.16: The ice floes disrupt its course, the snow disappears in their flow.
6.17: In times of drought, they vanish; with the first heat, their bed dries up.
6.18: Along various paths their waters are lost, they evaporate into the air and they dry up.
6.19: The caravans of Thema relied on them, the travelers of Saba placed their hopes in them,
6.20: They are frustrated in their waiting, having arrived at their shores, they remain confused.
6.21: So I miss you at this hour; at the sight of misfortune, you flee in terror.
6.22: Did I tell you, "Give me something, share your possessions with me,
6.23: "Deliver me from the hand of the enemy, snatch me from the hand of the robbers?"»
6.24: Instruct me and I will listen in silence; show me where I have failed.
6.25: How powerful are fair words! But on what do you place your blame?
6.26: Do you want to censor words? Speeches that have escaped despair are prey to the wind.
6.27: Ah, you cast a net on an orphan, you dig a trap for your friend.
6.28: Now, deign to turn back to me and you will see if I am lying to your face.
6.29: Come back, don't be unfair, come back and my innocence will appear.
6.30: Is there iniquity on my tongue, or does my palate not know how to discern evil?

7.1: Man's life on earth is a time of service, and his days are like those of a mercenary.
7.2: Like a slave longs for shade, like a worker waits for his wages,
7.3: Thus I shared months of pain, and for my part, nights of suffering.
7.4: When I go to bed, I say, «When will I get up? When will the night end?» And I am filled with anxieties until daybreak.
7.5: My flesh is covered with worms and an earthy crust, my skin is cracking and oozing.
7.6: My days pass faster than the weaver's shuttle, they vanish: no more hope.
7.7: O God, remember that my life is but a breath. My eyes will never see happiness again.
7.8: The eye that looks at me will no longer see me; your eye will search for me, and I will be no more.
7.9: The cloud dissipates and passes, so he who descends into Sheol will never return.,
7.10: He will never return to his home; the place he lived in will no longer recognize him.
7.11: Therefore I will not restrain my tongue, I will speak in the anguish of my spirit, I will vent my complaints in the bitterness of my soul.
7.12: Am I the sea or a sea monster, that you put a barrier around me?
7.13: When I say, "My bed will comfort me, my couch will calm my sighs,"«
7.14: So you frighten me with dreams, you terrify me with visions.
7.15: Ah, my soul prefers violent death, my bones call for demise.
7.16: I am in the throes of dissolution, life is slipping away from me forever, leave me, for my days are but a breath.
7.17: What is man, that you hold him in such high esteem, that you deign to take care of him?,
7.18: that you visit it every morning and experience it at every moment?
7.19: When will you stop staring at me? When will you give me time to swallow?
7.20: If I have sinned, what can I do to you, O Guardian of men? Why make myself a target for your arrows and a burden to myself?
7.21: Why do you not forgive my offense? Why do you not forget my iniquity? For soon I will sleep in the dust; you will seek me, but I will be no more.

8.1: Then Baldad of Suhe spoke and said:
8.2: How long will you continue these speeches, and will your words be like a breath of storm?
8.3: Does God bend justice, or does the Almighty overturn justice?
8.4: If your sons have sinned against him, he has delivered them into the hands of their iniquity.
8.5: For you, if you turn to God, if you implore the Almighty,
8.6: If you are upright and pure, then he will watch over you; he will restore happiness to the dwelling place of your righteousness.,
8.7: Your first state will seem insignificant, so flourishing will the second be.
8.8: Ask questions of past generations, pay attention to the experience of your fathers:
8.9: For we are of yesterday and know nothing; our days on earth pass like a shadow.,
8.10: Won't they teach you, speak to you, and draw out sentences from their hearts?
8.11: «"Does papyrus grow outside of marshes? Does rush grow without water?"
8.12: While still tender, without being cut, it dries before any grass.
8.13: Such are the ways of all who forget God; the hope of the wicked will perish.
8.14: His confidence will be shattered, his assurance is like a spider's web.
8.15: He leans on his house and it doesn't hold, he clings to it and it doesn't stay standing.
8.16: It is full of vigor, in the sun, its branches spread over its garden,
8.17: Its roots intertwine among the stones, it plunges to the depths of the rock.
8.18: If God snatches him from his place, his place denies him: I have never seen you.
8.19: That is where his joy ends, and from the same soil others will rise after him.»
8.20: No, God does not reject the innocent, he does not take the hand of evildoers.
8.21: He will fill your mouth with laughter and put songs of joy on your lips.
8.22: Your enemies will be covered in shame and the tent of the wicked will disappear.

9.1: Then Job spoke up and said:
9.2: I know that this is the case: how could man be just towards God?
9.3: If he wanted to argue with him, he wouldn't be able to answer a single one of a thousand things.
9.4: God is wise in heart and mighty in power: who has resisted him and remained at peace?
9.5: He moves mountains without their knowledge; he overturns them in his anger.,
9.6: It shakes the earth at its base and its pillars are shaken.
9.7: He commands the sun and the sun does not rise; he seals up the stars.
9.8: Alone, he stretches out the heavens, he walks on the heights of the sea.
9.9: He created the Great Bear, Orion, the Pleiades, and the southern sky regions.
9.10: He performs wonders that cannot be fathomed, prodigies that cannot be counted.
9.11: Here he is passing near me and I don't see him, he moves away without my noticing him.
9.12: If he seizes prey, who will oppose him, who will say to him, "What are you doing?"«
9.13: God, nothing softens his anger, before him the legions of pride bow down.
9.14: And I would think about how to respond to him, how to choose my words to discuss with him.
9.15: If justice were on my side, I would not respond; I would implore the clemency of my judge.
9.16: Even if he had responded to my call, I wouldn't believe he would have listened to my voice:
9.17: He who shatters me as if in a whirlwind and multiplies my wounds without reason,
9.18: which doesn't let me breathe and fills me with bitterness.
9.19: If it is a matter of force, behold, he is strong; if it is a matter of right, he says, "Who is suing me?"«
9.20: Even if I were irreproachable, my own mouth would condemn me; even if I were innocent, it would declare me perverse.
9.21: I am innocent, I do not care about existence and life is a burden to me.
9.22: It matters to me after all, that's why I said, "He destroys both the righteous and the wicked."«
9.23: If only the plague killed instantly. Alas, it mocks the trials of the innocent.
9.24: The earth is given into the hands of the wicked; God hides the face of his judges: if it is not he, who then is it?
9.25: My days are faster than a letter; they flee without having seen happiness.,
9.26: They pass by like a reed boat, like an eagle swooping down on its prey.
9.27: If I say, "I want to forget my complaint, to leave my sad expression behind, to adopt a joyful expression,"«
9.28: I tremble for all my pain, I know you won't hold me innocent.
9.29: I will be found guilty: why take an unnecessary sentence?
9.30: When I wash myself in the snow, when I purify my hands with soda,
9.31: You would plunge me into the mud and I would loathe my clothes.
9.32: God is not a man like me, so that I should answer him, so that we should appear together in court.
9.33: There is no referee between us who lays his hand on the two of us.
9.34: Let him remove his staff from me, let his terrors cease to frighten me:
9.35: So I will speak without fear, otherwise I am not myself.

10.1: My soul is weary of life, I will give free rein to my complaint, I will speak in the bitterness of my heart.
10.2: I say to God: Do not condemn me, teach me what you are accusing me of.
10.3: Do you find pleasure in oppressing, in rejecting the work of your hands, in showing favor to the counsel of the wicked?
10.4: Do you have eyes of flesh, or do you see as men see?
10.5: Are your days like the days of man, or your years like the years of a mortal?,
10.6: so that you may seek out my iniquity, so that you may pursue my sin,
10.7: When you know that I am not guilty and that no one can deliver me from your hand?
10.8: Your hands formed and shaped me, entirely, and you would destroy me.
10.9: Remember that you molded me like clay: and you would return me to dust.
10.10: Didn't you melt me like milk and curdle me like cheese?
10.11: You clothed me with skin and flesh, you wove me together with bones and sinews.
10.12: With life, you have granted me your favor and your providence has preserved my soul.
10.13: And yet, this is what you were hiding in your heart: I see clearly what you were plotting.
10.14: If I sin, you watch me; you do not forgive my iniquity.
10.15: Am I guilty? Woe to me. Am I innocent? I dare not raise my head, filled with shame and seeing my misery.
10.16: If I get up, you chase me like a lion, you start tormenting me strangely again.,
10.17: You bring new witnesses against me, you redouble your fury against me, fresh troops come to assail me.
10.18: Why did you take me from my mother's breast? I would have died and no eye would have seen me.
10.19: I would be as if I had never existed, as if I had been carried from my mother's womb to the tomb.
10.20: Are my days not so short? Let him leave me. Let him withdraw and let me breathe for a moment.,
10.21: before I depart, never to return, into the region of darkness and the shadow of death,
10.22: a bleak and gloomy region, where the shadow of death and chaos reign, where light is like darkness.

11.1: Then Zophar the Naamathite spoke up and said:
11.2: Will the multitude of words remain unanswered, and will the chatterer be right?
11.3: Will your empty words silence people? Will you mock, and no one will confound you?
11.4: You said to God, "My thoughts are true, and I am blameless before you."«
11.5: Oh, if God would speak, if he would open his lips to answer you,
11.6: If he were to reveal to you the secrets of his wisdom, the hidden recesses of his designs, you would then see that he forgets a part of your crimes.
11.7: Do you presume to fathom the depths of God, to reach the perfection of the Almighty?
11.8: It is as high as the heavens: what will you do? Deeper than the grave: what will you know?
11.9: Its length is greater than the earth, it is wider than the sea.
11.10: If he pounces on the culprit, if he arrests him, if he summons the court, who will oppose it?
11.11: Because he knows the perverse, he discovers iniquity before she suspects it.
11.12: At this sight, even the madman would understand, and the young wild ass would become reasonable.
11.13: For you, if you direct your heart towards God and stretch out your arms towards him,
11.14: If you remove the iniquity that is in your hands and do not allow injustice to dwell in your tent,
11.15: Then you will lift up your spotless forehead, you will be steadfast and you will fear no more.
11.16: Then you will forget your suffering, you will remember it like waters that have flowed away.,
11.17: The future will dawn for you brighter than midday, darkness will turn into sunrise.
11.18: You will be full of confidence and your waiting will not be in vain, you will look around you and you will go to bed peacefully.
11.19: You will rest, without anyone worrying you, and many will caress your face.
11.20: But the eyes of the wicked will burn out: for them, there is no refuge, their hope is the breath of a dying man.

12.1: Then Job spoke up and said:
12.2: Truly you are as wise as an entire people, and with you wisdom will die.
12.3: I too have intelligence like you, I don't give you any less in that regard, and who doesn't know the things you say?
12.4: I am the laughingstock of my friends, I who invoked God and to whom God answered, their laughingstock, I the righteous, the innocent.
12.5: Shame on misfortune. That's the motto of the fortunate; contempt awaits the one whose foot falters.
12.6: Peace, however, reigns in the tent of the robbers, security for those who provoke God and who have no other god than their own arm.
12.7: But please ask the animals and they will teach you, the birds of the air and they will tell you,
12.8: Or speak to the earth and it will teach you; even the fish of the sea will tell you.
12.9: Who among all these beings does not know that the hand of the Lord has done these things?,
12.10: that he holds in his hand the soul of all that lives and the breath of all humans?
12.11: Does not the ear discern words, as the palate savors food?
12.12: Wisdom belongs to white hair, prudence is the fruit of long days.
12.13: In God reside wisdom and power; to Him belong counsel and understanding.
12.14: Behold, he overthrows and it cannot be rebuilt; he shuts the door on man and it cannot be opened to him.
12.15: Behold, he stops the waters, they dry up; he releases them, they overturn the earth.
12.16: To Him belongs strength and prudence, to Him belongs the one who is led astray and the one who leads astray.
12.17: He takes captive the advisors of the people and he deprives judges of their senses.
12.18: He loosens the kings' belts and girds their loins with a rope.
12.19: He drags the priests into captivity and overthrows the powerful.
12.20: He takes away the speech of the most capable men and he takes away the judgment of the elderly.
12.21: He heaps contempt upon the nobles and loosens the belt of the strong.
12.22: It brings to light the things hidden in darkness and produces the shadow of death.
12.23: He makes nations grow and he destroys them, he expands them and he shrinks them.
12.24: He takes away the intelligence of the leaders of the peoples of the earth and leads them astray in trackless deserts,
12.25: They grope in the darkness, far from the light; he makes them wander like a drunken man.

13.1: Now my eye has seen all this, my ear has heard and understood it.
13.2: What you know, I know too; I am in no way inferior to you.
13.3: But I want to speak to the Almighty, I want to plead my case with God.
13.4: Because you are nothing but charlatans, you are all useless doctors.
13.5: Why didn't you remain silent? It would have served as wisdom for you.
13.6: Listen, I beg you, to my defense; pay attention to the plea from my lips.
13.7: Will you speak lies on behalf of God, for him? Will you speak deceit?
13.8: Will you show bias in favor of God? Will you be his advocates?
13.9: Will he be grateful if he searches your hearts? Will you deceive him as one deceives a man?
13.10: He will certainly condemn you if you secretly show bias.
13.11: Yes, his majesty will terrify you, his terrors will fall upon you.
13.12: Your arguments are reasons of dust, your fortresses are fortresses of clay.
13.13: Be quiet, leave me alone, I want to speak, whatever happens to me will happen.
13.14: I want to take my flesh between my teeth, I want to put my soul in my hand.
13.15: Even if he were to kill me, and I had nothing left to hope for, I would defend my conduct before him.
13.16: But he will be my salvation, for the wicked cannot appear in his presence.
13.17: Listen to my words, pay attention to my speech.
13.18: Here I have prepared my case, I know I will be vindicated.
13.19: Is there anyone who wants to plead against me? Right now I want to be silent and die.
13.20: Only spare me two things, O God, and I will not hide from your face:
13.21: Keep your hand away from me and let your terrors no longer frighten me.
13.22: After that, call and I will answer, or I will speak first and you can answer me.
13.23: What is the number of my iniquities and my sins? Make me know my transgressions and my offenses.
13.24: Why hide your face like that and look at me as your enemy?.
13.25: Do you want to frighten a leaf stirred by the wind, chase after a dried-out straw,
13.26: so that you will write bitter things against me, so that you will blame me for the faults of my youth,
13.27: so that you may place my feet in wooden shackles, observe my every step, and mark a boundary on the soles of my feet,
13.28: while my body is being consumed like rotten wood, like a garment devoured by moths.

14.1: The man born of woman lives a few days and is filled with miseries.
14.2: Like a flower, it is born and cut down; it flees like a shadow, without stopping.
14.3: And it is on him that you have your eye, him that you bring to justice with you.
14.4: Who can separate the pure from the impure? No one.
14.5: If man's days are numbered, if you have fixed the number of his months, if you have set a limit he must not exceed,
14.6: Turn your eyes away from him so that he may rest, until he tastes, like the worker, the end of his day.
14.7: A tree has hope: cut down, it can still turn green again, it does not cease to produce offshoots.
14.8: Whether its root has aged in the earth, whether its trunk has died in the dust,
14.9: As soon as it smells water, it turns green again, it puts out branches like a young plant.
14.10: But when a man dies and lies there, when he has expired, where is he?
14.11: The lake's waters disappear, the river dries up and becomes dry:
14.12: Thus the man lies down and does not rise again; he will not awaken as long as the sky remains; he cannot be roused from his sleep.
14.13: Oh, if you would hide me in the realm of the dead, keep me hidden there until your anger has passed, set a time for me when you would remember me.
14.14: If a man could live again once he died. All my time in service I would wait to be relieved.
14.15: Then you would call me and I would answer you, you would long for the work of your hands.
14.16: But alas, now you count my steps, you have your eye open on my sins,
14.17: My transgressions are sealed in a bag, and you put a coating over my iniquities.
14.18: The mountain collapses and disappears, the rock is carried out of its place,
14.19: The waters wear away the stone, their overflowing floods carry away the dust of the ground: thus you annihilate the hope of man.
14.20: You strike him down and he goes away, you disgrace his face and you dismiss him.
14.21: Whether his children are honored, he does not know; whether they are in abasement, he is unaware.
14.22: His flesh feels only its own suffering, his soul groans only for itself.

15.1: Then Eliphaz the Temanite spoke up and said:
15.2: Does the wise man respond with empty knowledge? Does he inflate his chest with wind?
15.3: Is he defending himself with futile remarks, with speeches that serve no purpose?
15.4: You destroy even the fear of God, you annihilate all piety towards God.
15.5: Your mouth reveals your iniquity, and you adopt the language of the deceitful.
15.6: It is not I, it is your mouth that condemns you, it is your lips that testify against you.
15.7: Were you born the first of men? Were you brought forth before the hills?
15.8: Have you attended God's council? Have you stolen wisdom for yourself alone?
15.9: What do you know that we do not know? What have you learned that is not familiar to us?
15.10: We also have among us white hair, old men richer in years than your father.
15.11: Do you consider God's consolations and the gentle words we address to you to be of little value?
15.12: Where does your heart take you, and what does that rolling of your eyes mean?
15.13: What, is it against God that you turn your anger and that you utter such words from your mouth?
15.14: What is man, that he should be pure, or the son of woman, that he should be righteous?
15.15: Behold, God does not trust even his saints, and the heavens are not pure before him:
15.16: How much less this abominable and perverse being, the man who drinks iniquity like water.
15.17: I will instruct you, listen to me, I will tell you what I have seen,
15.18: What the wise teach, they do not hide, having learned it from their fathers.,
15.19: The country had been given to them alone, and no foreigner ever passed among them.
15.20: «"The wicked man is consumed by anguish throughout his days, but only a few years are reserved for the oppressor.".
15.21: Terrifying noises resound in his ears; in the midst of peace, the destroyer bears down upon him.
15.22: He does not hope to escape the darkness; he feels that he is being watched for the sword.
15.23: He wanders in search of his bread, he knows that the day of darkness is coming, at his side.
15.24: Distress and anguish fall upon him, they assail him like a king armed for battle.
15.25: For he has raised his hand against God, he has defied the Almighty,
15.26: He ran towards him with his neck stiff, beneath the thick back of his shields.
15.27: His face was covered in grease and his sides were heavy with fat.
15.28: He occupied cities that no longer exist, houses that no longer have inhabitants, destined to become heaps of stone.
15.29: He will not grow richer, his fortune will not last, his possessions will no longer extend over the earth.
15.30: He will not escape the darkness, the flame will wither his offspring, and he will be carried away by the breath of God's mouth.
15.31: Let him not hope for anything from the lie, he will be caught in it, the lie will be his reward.
15.32: She will arrive before her days are full and her branch will no longer turn green.
15.33: It will shake off, like the vine, its fruit as soon as it blossoms, it will drop its flower, like the olive tree.
15.34: For the house of the wicked is barren, and fire devours the tent of the corrupt judge.
15.35: He conceived evil and he gives birth to misfortune; in his womb ripens a fruit of deception.»

16.1: Then Job spoke up and said:
16.2: I have often heard similar harangues; you are all unbearable comforters.
16.3: When will these empty speeches end? What spurs you to reply?
16.4: I too would know how to speak like you; if you were in my place, I would arrange beautiful speeches for you, I would nod my head at you.,
16.5: I would encourage you with my mouth, and you would find relief in the restlessness of my lips.
16.6: If I speak, my pain is not eased; if I remain silent, is it relieved?
16.7: Today, alas, God has exhausted my strength, O God, you have reaped all my loved ones.
16.8: You strangle me, it's a testimony against me, my thinness rises up against me, facing me it accuses me.
16.9: His anger tears me apart and pursues me, he gnashes his teeth at me, my enemy glares at me.
16.10: They open their mouths to devour me, they strike me on the cheek with outrage, they all conspire together to destroy me.
16.11: God has delivered me to the perverse; he has thrown me into the hands of the wicked.
16.12: I was at peace, and he shook me, he seized me by the nape of the neck and he broke me. He set me as an object to his arrows,
16.13: His arrows fly around me, he pierces my sides without pity, he spills my entrails on the earth,
16.14: He is breaching me breach after breach, he is bearing down on me like a giant.
16.15: I sewed a bag onto my skin and rolled my forehead in the dust.
16.16: My face is red with tears and the shadow of death spreads across my eyelids.,
16.17: although there is no iniquity in my hands and my prayer is pure.
16.18: O earth, do not cover my blood and let my cries rise freely.
16.19: At this very hour, behold, I have my witness in heaven, my defender in the high places.
16.20: My friends mock me, but my eyes weep for God.
16.21: Let him judge for himself between God and man, between the son of man and his fellow man
16.22: because the years allotted to me are passing by and I am entering a path from which I will not return.

17.1: My breath is failing, my days are fading away, all that remains for me is the grave.
17.2: I am surrounded by mockers, my eye watches over them amidst their insults.
17.3: O God, be my guarantor before yourself: who else would strike me on the hand?
17.4: Because you have closed their hearts to wisdom, do not allow them to exalt themselves.
17.5: He invites his friends to share, when his children's eyes fail him.
17.6: He has made me the laughingstock of the people; I am the man who is spat upon.
17.7: My eyes are clouded by grief and all my limbs are but a shadow.
17.8: Upright men are astonished, and the innocent are angered against the impious.
17.9: The righteous man, however, remains steadfast in his way, and he who has clean hands redoubles his courage.
17.10: But all of you, come back, come then, shall I not find a wise man among you?
17.11: My days are over, my plans destroyed, those plans that my heart cherished.
17.12: From the night they make day; in the face of darkness, they say that light is near.
17.13: Though I wait, Sheol is my dwelling place; in darkness I have made my bed.
17.14: I said to the grave, "You are my father," to the worms, "You are my mother and my sister."«
17.15: Where is my hope? Who can see my hope?
17.16: She descended to the gates of Sheol, if indeed one finds rest in the dust.

18.1: Then Baldad of Suhe spoke and said:
18.2: When will you put an end to this talk? Be intelligent, and then we'll talk.
18.3: Why do you look at us like brutes and are we stupid in your eyes?
18.4: You who tear yourself apart in your fury, do you want the earth to become desolate because of you, and the rock to be removed from its place?
18.5: Yes, the light of the wicked will be extinguished and the flame of his hearth will cease to shine.
18.6: The day will darken beneath his tent, his lamp will go out above him.
18.7: His firm steps will be cramped, his own advice hastens his downfall.
18.8: His feet throw him into the net; he steps on the trap.
18.9: The net caught his heels; he was tightly bound in its knots.
18.10: For him, the lakes are hidden underground and the trapdoor is on his path.
18.11: Terrors besiege him from all sides and pursue him step by step.
18.12: Famine is his punishment and ruin is ready for his downfall.
18.13: The skin of his limbs is devoured, his limbs are devoured by the firstborn of death.
18.14: He is dragged from his tent, where he thought he was safe, and taken to the King of Terrors.
18.15: None of his people dwell in his tent; sulfur is sown on his dwelling.
18.16: At the bottom, its roots are drying out; at the top, its branches are being cut.
18.17: His memory has vanished from the earth; he no longer has a name in the land.
18.18: We chase him from the light into the darkness, we banish him from the universe.
18.19: He left no descendants or posterity in his tribe, no survivors in his sojourn.
18.20: The peoples of the West are astonished by its ruin, and those of the East are filled with horror.
18.21: Such is the dwelling place of the wicked, such is the place of the man who does not know God.

19.1: Then Job spoke up and said:
19.2: How long will you afflict my soul and overwhelm me with your words?
19.3: This is the tenth time you have insulted me, shamelessly outraged me.
19.4: Even if I had failed, the fault would still lie with me.
19.5: But you, who rise up against me, who invoke my disgrace to convince me,
19.6: Finally, know that it is God who oppresses me and who enfolds me in his net.
19.7: Here I am, crying out against violence and no one answers me. I appeal for help and there is no justice.
19.8: He has blocked my path and I cannot pass: he has spread darkness on my ways.
19.9: He has stripped me of my glory, he has taken the crown from my head.
19.10: He has undermined me from all sides and I am falling; he has uprooted my hope, like a tree.
19.11: His anger was kindled against me; he treated me like his enemies.
19.12: His battalions came together, they fought their way to me, they are laying siege to my tent.
19.13: He has distanced my brothers from me, and my friends have turned away from me.
19.14: My loved ones have abandoned me, my close friends have forgotten me.
19.15: The guests in my house and my servants treat me like a stranger; I am a stranger in their eyes.
19.16: I call to my servant, but he does not answer me; I am reduced to begging him with my mouth.
19.17: My wife hates my breath, my own children are disgusted by me.
19.18: Even the children despise me; if I stand up, they mock me.
19.19: All those who were my confidants now hate me, those I loved have turned against me.
19.20: My bones are attached to my skin and flesh, I escaped with the skin of my teeth.
19.21: Have pity, have pity on me, you at least, my friends, for the hand of God has struck me.
19.22: Why do you pursue me, as God pursues me? Why are you insatiable for my flesh?
19.23: Oh, who will grant that my words be written down, who will grant that they be recorded in a book,
19.24: that with an iron chisel and lead, they may be forever engraved in the rock.
19.25: I know that my redeemer lives and that he will be the last to rise from the dust.
19.26: Then from this skeleton, clothed in its skin, in my flesh, I will see God.
19.27: I myself will see it, my eyes will see it and no other; my loins are consumed with expectation within me.
19.28: Then you will say, "Why were we pursuing him?" and the justice of my cause will be recognized.
19.29: On that day, fear the sword for yourselves: terrible are the vengeances of the sword. And you will know that there is justice.

20.1: Then Zophar the Naamathite spoke up and said:
20.2: That is why my thoughts suggest an answer to me, and because of my agitation, I am eager to give it.
20.3: I have heard reproaches that outrage me; in my intelligence, my mind will find the reply.
20.4: Do you know that, from time immemorial, ever since man was placed on Earth,
20.5: Was the triumph of the wicked short-lived, and the joy of the wicked only a moment?
20.6: Even if he were to raise his pride to the heavens and his head to touch the clouds,
20.7: Like his garbage, he perished forever; those who saw him said, "Where is he?"«
20.8: It flies away like a dream and is no longer to be found; it fades away like a vision of the night.
20.9: The eye that saw him can no longer see him, his dwelling will no longer perceive him.
20.10: His children will beg the poor, he will return his plunder with his own hands.
20.11: His bones were full of his hidden iniquities; they will sleep with him in the dust.
20.12: Because the evil was sweet to his mouth, because he hid it under his tongue,
20.13: that he savored it without abandoning it and kept it in the middle of his palate:
20.14: Her food will turn to poison in her guts, it will become the venom of the asp in her breast.
20.15: He has swallowed riches, he will vomit them up, God will remove them from his stomach.
20.16: He sucked the asp's venom, the viper's tongue will kill him.
20.17: He will never see the rivers flow, the torrents of honey and milk.
20.18: He will return what he has earned and will not gorge himself on it, in proportion to his profits, and he will not enjoy it.
20.19: For he has oppressed and abandoned the poor; he has plundered their homes and not rebuilt them.
20.20: His greed could not be satisfied; he will not take with him what he holds most dear.
20.21: Nothing escaped his voracious appetite, so his happiness will not last.
20.22: In the midst of abundance, he falls into want; all the blows of misfortune come upon him.
20.23: This is what will fill his stomach: God will send the fire of his anger upon him, and it will rain down upon him, even into his bowels.
20.24: If he escapes the iron weapons, the bronze bow pierces him.
20.25: He pulls out the arrow, it comes out of his body, steel gleams from his liver, the terrors of death fall upon him.
20.26: A deep night swallowed his treasures, a fire that the man did not light devoured him and consumed everything that remained in his tent.
20.27: The heavens will reveal his iniquity, and the earth will rise up against him.
20.28: The abundance of her house will be scattered; it will disappear on the day of wrath.
20.29: This is the portion that God reserves for the wicked and the inheritance that God destines for them.

21.1: Then Job spoke up and said:
21.2: Listen, listen to my words, so that I may at least have this consolation from you.
21.3: Allow me to speak in my turn, and when I have spoken, you can laugh.
21.4: Is my complaint directed against a man? How then could my patience not be failing me?
21.5: Look at me and be amazed and put your hand over your mouth.
21.6: When I think about it, I shudder and a shiver runs through my flesh.
21.7: Why do villains live and grow old, increasing in strength?
21.8: Their posterity is strengthened around them, their offspring flourish in their eyes.
21.9: Their home is at peace, safe from fear; the rod of God does not touch them.
21.10: Their bull is always fertile, their heifer gives birth and does not abort.
21.11: They let their children run around like a flock, their newborns bouncing around them.
21.12: They sing to the sound of the tambourine and the zither, they entertain themselves to the sound of the reed pipe.
21.13: They spend their days in happiness and in an instant they descend into Sheol.
21.14: Yet they said to God, «Depart from us, we do not desire to know your ways.
21.15: What is the Almighty, that we should serve Him? What would we gain by praying to Him?»
21.16: Is not their prosperity in their own hands? However, far be it from me to follow the advice of the wicked.
21.17: Do we often see the lamp of the wicked extinguished, ruin befall them, and God assign them a portion in his anger?
21.18: Do we see them as straw carried away by the wind, as chaff swept away by the whirlwind?
21.19: «"God, you say, reserves his punishment for his children." But let God punish him himself so that he may feel it.,
21.20: that he may see with his own eyes his ruin, that he may himself drink the wrath of the Almighty.
21.21: Indeed, what does his house matter to him after he dies, once the number of his months is decided?
21.22: Is it to God that wisdom will be taught, to him who judges the highest beings?
21.23: One dies in the midst of his prosperity, perfectly happy and peaceful.,
21.24: the flanks laden with fat and the bone marrow filled with sap.
21.25: The other dies, bitter in his soul, without having tasted happiness.
21.26: They both lie down in the dust and the worms cover them both.
21.27: Ah, I know very well what your thoughts are, what unfair judgments you make about me.
21.28: You say, «Where is the house of the oppressor? What has become of the tent inhabited by the wicked?»
21.29: Have you never questioned the travelers and are you unaware of their remarks?
21.30: In the day of misfortune, the wicked are spared; in the day of wrath, they escape punishment.
21.31: Who will condemn his conduct to his face? Who will hold him accountable for what he has done?
21.32: He is honorably carried to his tomb and his mausoleum is watched over.
21.33: The clods of earth in the valley are light to him and all the men follow him there, as countless generations have preceded him.
21.34: What then is the meaning of your vain consolations? All that remains of your answers is treachery.

22.1: Then Eliphaz spoke up and said:
22.2: Can man be useful to God? The wise man is only useful to himself.
22.3: What does it matter to the Almighty whether you are righteous? If you are upright in your ways, what does He gain?
22.4: Is it because of your piety that he punishes you, that he enters into judgment with you?
22.5: Is not your malice immense, your iniquities without measure?
22.6: You took pledges from your brothers without cause, you stripped the naked from those who were naked.
22.7: You did not give water to the exhausted man, you refused bread to the hungry.
22.8: The land belonged to the strongest arm, and the protected one established his dwelling there.
22.9: You sent widows away empty-handed, and the arms of orphans were broken.
22.10: That's why you are surrounded by traps and troubled by sudden terrors,
22.11: in the midst of darkness, unable to see and submerged by the flood of waters.
22.12: Is not God in the highest heavens? Look at the faces of the stars: how high they are!.
22.13: And you said, "What does God know? Can he judge through the deep clouds?"
22.14: The clouds form a veil over him, and he cannot see; he wanders on the circle of the sky.»
22.15: Do you then keep to the ancient ways, where men of iniquity walked?,
22.16: which were swept away before their time, whose foundations were torn away by the waters.
22.17: They were the ones who said to God, "Get away from us. What could the Almighty do to us?"«
22.18: Yet it was he who had filled their homes with riches. Far be it from me to follow the counsel of the wicked.
22.19: The righteous see their downfall and rejoice, the innocent mock them:
22.20: «"There are our enemies destroyed. Fire has devoured their riches."»
22.21: Therefore, reconcile yourself with God and find peace, and happiness will be restored to you.
22.22: Receive instruction from his mouth and put his words in your heart.
22.23: You will rise again if you return to the Almighty, if you remove iniquity from your tent.
22.24: Throw the gold ingots into the dust and the gold of Ophir among the pebbles of the stream.
22.25: And the Almighty will be your gold, he will be a heap of silver for you.
22.26: Then you will find your delight in the Almighty and lift up your face to Him.
22.27: You will pray to him and he will listen to you, and you will fulfill your vows.
22.28: If you form a plan, it will succeed, and light will shine on your paths.
22.29: To bowed heads you will cry: "Up!" and God will help the one whose eyes are downcast.
22.30: He will even deliver the guilty, saved by the purity of your hands.

23.1: Then Job spoke up and said:
23.2: Yes, today my complaint is bitter, and yet my hand holds back my sighs.
23.3: Oh, who will tell me where to find him, how to reach his throne.
23.4: I would plead my case before him and fill my mouth with arguments.
23.5: I would know the reasons he might give me, I would see what he might have to say to me.
23.6: Would he oppose me with the grandeur of his power? Wouldn't he at least glance at me?
23.7: Then the innocent man would argue with him, and I would leave, absolved forever by my judge.
23.8: But if I go to the east, he is not there; in the west, I do not see him.
23.9: Is he occupied in the north? I cannot see him; is he hiding in the south? I cannot discover him.
23.10: However, he knows the paths I walk; let him examine me, and I shall emerge pure as gold.
23.11: My foot has always trodden in his footsteps, I have stayed in his path without deviating.
23.12: I have not deviated from the precepts of his lips; I have submitted my will to the words of his mouth.
23.13: But he has one thought: who will bring him back? What he desires, he does.
23.14: He will therefore accomplish what he has decreed concerning me, and he has many such plans.
23.15: That's why I get flustered in his presence; when I think about it, I'm afraid of him.
23.16: God melts my heart, the Almighty fills me with awe.
23.17: For it is not darkness that consumes me, nor the gloom that veils my face.

24.1: Why has the Almighty not set aside days when he would exercise his judgment, when his faithful would see him intervene?
24.2: We see men moving boundary markers, grazing the flock they stole.
24.3: They drive the orphan's donkey before them and hold the widow's ox as a pledge.
24.4: They force the poor to turn away from the path; all the humble people of the country are reduced to hiding.
24.5: Like the wild ass in its solitude, they go out to work early in the morning, searching for their food. The desert provides sustenance for their children.,
24.6: They cut the ears of grain in the fields, they plunder in the vineyard of their oppressor.
24.7: Naked, they spend the night, lacking clothes and without blankets against the cold.
24.8: The mountain rain penetrates them; lacking shelter, they huddle against the rock.
24.9: They snatch the orphan from the breast, they take pledges from the poor.
24.10: These men, completely naked and without clothes, carry, starving, the master's sheaves of grain.,
24.11: They press the oil in its cellars, they tread the grapes, and they are thirsty.
24.12: From the heart of the cities rise the groans of men and the souls of the wounded cry out, and God does not heed these crimes.
24.13: Others are among the enemies of the light; they do not know its ways, they do not stand in its paths.
24.14: The assassin rises at daybreak, he kills the poor and the needy, he prowls at night like a thief.
24.15: The eye of the adulterer spies on the twilight, "No one sees me," he says, and he throws a veil over his face.
24.16: At night, others break into houses; by day, they remain hidden: they do not know the light.
24.17: For them, morning is like the shadow of death, for the horrors of the night are familiar to them.
24.18: Ah, the wicked man glides like a light body over the face of the waters; he has only a cursed portion on the earth; he does not walk on the path of the vineyards.
24.19: Just as drought and heat absorb the water from the snows, so Sheol swallows up sinners.
24.20: Ah, the mother's breast forgets him, the worms delight in him, he is no longer remembered, and iniquity is broken like a tree.
24.21: He devoured the barren and childless woman, and he did no good to the widow.
24.22: But God, by his power, shakes the mighty; he rises up, and they no longer rely on life.,
24.23: He gives them security and confidence, and his eyes watch over their paths.
24.24: They rose up and in an instant they are no more, they fall, they are harvested like all men, they are cut down like the heads of the ears of corn.
24.25: If it is not so, who will convict me of lying? Who will render my words meaningless?

25.1: Then Baldad of Suhe spoke and said:
25.2: Domination and terror belong to him, he establishes peace in his lofty dwellings.
25.3: Are not his legions countless? Upon whom does his light not rise?
25.4: How can a man be righteous before God? How can the son of a woman be pure?
25.5: Even the moon is without light, and the stars are not pure in its eyes:
25.6: How much less man, that worm, the son of man, that vile insect.

26.1: Then Job spoke up and said:
26.2: As you know how to help the weak, to lend aid to the powerless arm.
26.3: How well you advise the ignorant. What an abundance of wisdom you display.
26.4: To whom do you speak? And whose spirit is that which comes from your mouth?
26.5: Before God, the shadows tremble beneath the waters and their inhabitants.
26.6: Sheol is laid bare before him, and the abyss has no veil.
26.7: He extends the north over the void, he suspends the earth over nothingness.
26.8: It contains the waters in its clouds, and the clouds do not tear under their weight.
26.9: He veils the face of his throne, he spreads his clouds over it.
26.10: He drew a circle on the surface of the waters, at the point where light and darkness were divided.
26.11: The pillars of heaven tremble and recoil in terror at his threat.
26.12: By his power he stirs the sea, by his wisdom he breaks pride.
26.13: By his breath the sky becomes serene, his hand has formed the fleeing serpent.
26.14: Such are the edges of its paths, the faint murmur we perceive from them, but the thunder of its power, who will be able to hear it?

27.1: Job resumed his speech and said:
27.2: By the living God who denies me justice, by the Almighty who fills my soul with bitterness:
27.3: As long as I have breath, as long as the breath of God is in my nostrils,
27.4: My lips will not utter anything unjust, my tongue will not speak a lie.
27.5: Far be it from me to admit you're right. Until my dying breath, I will defend my innocence.
27.6: I have undertaken my justification, I will not abandon it, my heart does not condemn any of my days.
27.7: May my enemy be treated like the wicked. May my adversary suffer the fate of the impious.
27.8: What hope will the wicked have when God cuts him off, when He takes away his soul?
27.9: Will God listen to his cries on the day when anguish comes upon him?
27.10: Does he find his delight in the Almighty? Does he always address his prayers to God?
27.11: I will teach you God's ways and I will not hide from you the plans of the Almighty.
27.12: Look, you yourselves have all seen it, so why are you talking in vain?
27.13: This is the portion that God reserves for the wicked, the inheritance that the violent receive from the Almighty.
27.14: If he has many sons, it is for the sword; his offspring will not be satisfied with bread.
27.15: Those who survive will be buried in death, and their widows will not mourn them.
27.16: If he amasses silver like dust, if he piles up clothes like mud,
27.17: He is the one who hoards, but the righteous one carries them; the righteous one inherits your money.
27.18: His house is like the one built by the moth, like the hut built by the vineyard keeper.
27.19: The rich man goes to bed, it's for the last time, he opens his eyes, he is no more.
27.20: Terrors descend upon him like waters, a whirlwind sweeps him away in the middle of the night.
27.21: The east wind carries it away and it disappears, violently tearing it from its home.
27.22: God hurls his arrows at him without mercy; he flees in terror from his hand.,
27.23: People clap their hands over him, and hiss at him from their homes.

28.1: For silver there is a place from which it is extracted, for gold there is a place where it is refined.
28.2: Iron is extracted from the earth and molten stone yields copper.
28.3: Man puts an end to the darkness; he explores, to the very depths of the abyss, the stone hidden in the darkness and the shadow of death.
28.4: He digs, far from inhabited places, galleries, unknown to the feet of the living, suspended, he wavers, far from humans.
28.5: The earth, from which the bread comes, is shaken in its bowels as if by fire.
28.6: Its rocks are the source of sapphires and gold dust is found there.
28.7: The bird of prey does not know the path, the vulture's eye has not seen it.
28.8: Wild animals have not trodden upon it, the lion has never passed through it.
28.9: The man places his hand on the granite, he shakes the mountains to their roots.
28.10: He bores tunnels through the rocks; nothing precious escapes his gaze.
28.11: He knows how to stop the seepage of water, he brings to light everything that was hidden.
28.12: But where can Wisdom be found? Where is the place of Intelligence?
28.13: Man does not know its price; it is not found on the land of the living.
28.14: The abyss says, "She is not in my bosom," and the sea says, "She is not with me."«
28.15: It cannot be given for pure gold, it cannot be bought by the weight of silver.
28.16: It cannot be compared with Ophir gold, precious onyx, or sapphire.
28.17: Gold and glass cannot be compared to it; it is not exchanged for a vase of fine gold.
28.18: Let no mention be made of coral and crystal: the possession of wisdom is better than pearls.
28.19: Ethiopian topaz cannot equal it, and pure gold cannot reach its value.
28.20: Where does wisdom come from? Where does intelligence reside?
28.21: It is hidden from the eyes of all living things, it is hidden from the birds of the sky.
28.22: Hell and death say, "We've heard about it."«
28.23: God knows her way; he knows where she resides.
28.24: For he sees to the ends of the earth, he perceives all that is under heaven.
28.25: When he regulated the weight of the winds, when he balanced the waters,
28.26: when he gave laws to the rain, when he traced the path for the lightning bolts,
28.27: So he saw it and described it, he established it and explored its secrets.
28.28: Then he said to the man, “The fear of the Lord, that is wisdom, and fleeing from evil, that is understanding.”.

29.1: Job resumed his speech and said:
29.2: Oh, who will give me back the months of yesteryear, the days when God watched over me?,
29.3: when his lamp shone upon my head and his light guided me through the darkness.
29.4: As I was in the days of my mature years, when God visited me familiarly in my tent,
29.5: when the Almighty was still with me and my sons were around me,
29.6: when I washed my feet in milk and the rock poured streams of oil on me.
29.7: When I went out to the city gate and set up my seat in the public square,
29.8: When they saw me, the young men hid, the old men got up and stood.
29.9: The princes held their tongues and put their hands over their mouths.
29.10: The leaders' voices remained silent; their tongues clung to their palates.
29.11: The ear that heard me proclaimed me happy, the eye that saw me bore witness to me.
29.12: For I saved the poor who cried out for help and the orphan who had no support.
29.13: The blessing of the one who was about to perish came upon me; I filled the widow's heart with joy.
29.14: I put on righteousness as a garment; my justice was my cloak and my turban.
29.15: I was the eye of the blind man and the foot of the lame man.
29.16: I was the father of the poor, I carefully examined the cause of the unknown.
29.17: I broke the jaw of the unjust man and snatched his prey from between his teeth.
29.18: I used to say: "I will die in my nest, I will have many days like the sand.
29.19: My roots extend towards the waters, the dew spends the night in my foliage.
29.20: My glory will ever flourish again, and my bow will regain its strength in my hand.»
29.21: They listened to me and waited, they silently gathered my opinion.
29.22: After I had spoken, no one added anything; my words flowed over them like dew.
29.23: They were waiting for me like one waits for rain, they opened their mouths as if for spring showers.
29.24: If I smiled at them, they couldn't believe it; they eagerly received this sign of favor.
29.25: When I went to them, I had the first place, I sat like a king surrounded by his troops, like a comforter in the midst of the afflicted.

30.1: And now I am the laughingstock of men younger than me, whose fathers I would not have deigned to put among the dogs of my flock.
30.2: What would I have done with the strength of their arms? They are deprived of all vigor.
30.3: Dried out by misery and hunger, they graze in the desert, a land that has long been arid and desolate.
30.4: They pick bitter buds from the bushes, their only bread is the root of the broom.
30.5: They are excluded from human society, and people shout at them as if they were thieves.
30.6: They live in dreadful valleys, in caverns in the earth and rocks.
30.7: Their wild cries can be heard among the undergrowth; they lie down together under the brambles:
30.8: senseless people, nameless race, banished with contempt from the inhabited earth.
30.9: And now I am the subject of their songs, I am the target of their words.
30.10: They hate me, they flee from me, they do not turn their spit away from my face.
30.11: They give themselves free rein to insult me, they reject all restraints in my face.
30.12: Wretches rise up at my right, they seek to shake my feet, they blaze their murderous paths right up to me.
30.13: They have disrupted my paths, they are working towards my ruin, they whom no one would help.
30.14: They rush towards me, as if through a wide breach, they plunge through the rubble.
30.15: Terrors besiege me, my prosperity is swept away like a breath, my happiness has passed like a cloud.
30.16: And now my soul pours out within me; the days of affliction have seized me.
30.17: The night pierces my bones, consumes them, the evil that gnaws at me does not sleep.
30.18: Because of its violence, my garment has lost its shape; it fits me like a tunic.
30.19: God has thrown me into the mire; I am like dust and ashes.
30.20: I shout to you and you don't answer me, I stand there and you look at me with indifference.
30.21: You are becoming cruel towards me, you are attacking me with the full force of your arm.
30.22: You take me away, you make me fly with the wind and you annihilate me in the crash of the storm.
30.23: Because, I know, you are leading me to death, to the rendezvous of all the living.
30.24: However, will not the one who is about to perish stretch out his hands and cry out in his distress?
30.25: Did I not shed tears for the unfortunate man? Did my heart not soften for the destitute?
30.26: I was waiting for happiness and misfortune arrived, I was hoping for light and darkness came.
30.27: My insides are constantly boiling, the days of affliction have descended upon me.
30.28: I walk in mourning, without sunshine. If I rise in the assembly, it is to shout.
30.29: I have become the brother of jackals, the companion of ostrich daughters.
30.30: My livid skin is falling away in shreds, my bones are burned by an inner fire.
30.31: My zither now produces only mournful chords, my reed pipe only plaintive sounds.

31.1: I had made a pact with my eyes, and how could I have stopped my gaze on a virgin?.
31.2: What portion, I asked myself, would God reserve for me from above? What fate would the Almighty make for me from his heaven?
31.3: Is not ruin for the wicked and misfortune for the workers of iniquity?
31.4: Does not God know my ways? Does not he count all my steps?
31.5: If I have walked in the path of lies, if my foot has chased after fraud,
31.6: May God weigh me in just scales and he will recognize my innocence.
31.7: If my steps have strayed from the right path, if my heart has followed my eyes, if any defilement has clung to my hands,
31.8: that I sow and another eats, that my offspring be uprooted.
31.9: If my heart has been seduced by a woman, if I have watched at my neighbor's door,
31.10: that my wife grinds for another, that strangers dishonor her.
31.11: Because this is a horrible crime, an offense punishable by judges,
31.12: a fire that devours everything to the point of ruin, which would have destroyed all my possessions.
31.13: If I have disregarded the rights of my servant or my maidservant when they were in dispute with me:
31.14: What will I do when God rises? On the day of his visit, what will I answer him?
31.15: He who made me in my mother's womb, did he not also make me? Did not the same Creator form us?
31.16: If I have denied the poor what they desired, if I have caused the widow's eyes to languish,
31.17: if I ate my piece of bread alone, without the orphan having had his share:
31.18: From my childhood he looked after me like a father, from my birth he guided my steps.
31.19: If I have seen the unfortunate perish without clothing, the destitute without a blanket,
31.20: without his loins having blessed me, without the fleece of my lambs having warmed him,
31.21: If I raised my hand against the orphan because I saw myself as having support in the judges,
31.22: that my shoulder should detach from my torso, that my arm should be torn off at the elbow.
31.23: For I fear God's vengeance, and before his majesty I cannot stand.
31.24: If I have placed my trust in gold, if I have said to pure gold, "You are my hope,"«
31.25: if I rejoiced in the abundance of my possessions, in the treasures my hands had gathered,
31.26: if, seeing the sun cast its light and the moon advance in its splendor,
31.27: My heart was secretly seduced, if my hand went to my mouth,
31.28: This is yet another crime that the judge punishes; I would have denied the Most High God.
31.29: If I rejoiced at the ruin of my enemy, if I exulted when misfortune struck him:
31.30: No, I did not allow my tongue to sin, by calling for its death with an imprecation.
31.31: If the people in my tent didn't say, "Where can you find someone who isn't satisfied with their meal?"«
31.32: if the stranger spent the night outside, if I did not open the door to the traveler.
31.33: If I, like men, have disguised my faults and hidden my iniquities within myself,
31.34: out of fear of the large gathering, out of fear of the contempt of the families, to the point of remaining silent and not daring to cross the threshold of my door.
31.35: Oh, who will help me find someone who will listen to me? Here is my signature: May the Almighty answer me. May my adversary also write his indictment.
31.36: We'll see if I don't put it on my shoulder, if I don't encircle my brow with it like a diadem.
31.37: I will give an account to my judge of all my steps; I will approach him like a prince.
31.38: If my land cries out against me, if I have made its furrows weep,
31.39: If I ate her products without having paid for them, if I took them from their rightful owners,
31.40: that instead of wheat thorns should grow there, and weeds instead of barley. Here end the words of Job.

32.1: These three men stopped answering Job, because he persisted in considering himself righteous.
32.2: Then Eliu, son of Barachel the Buzite, of the family of Ram, became angry. His anger burned against Job because he claimed to be more righteous than God.
32.3: She also became angry with her three friends, because they had not found good answers to give her and yet they condemned Job.
32.4: Because they were older than him, Eliu had waited to speak to Job.
32.5: But seeing that there was no further response from the mouths of these three men, he flew into a rage.
32.6: Then Eliu, son of Barachel the Buzite, spoke up and said: I am young and you are old men, therefore I was afraid and dreaded to tell you my feelings.
32.7: I told myself, "Time will tell, and many years will reveal wisdom."«
32.8: But it is the spirit placed in man, the breath of the Almighty, that gives him intelligence.
32.9: It is not age that gives wisdom, nor is it old age that discerns justice.
32.10: That's why I say, "Listen to me, I too will express my thoughts."«
32.11: I waited while you spoke, I listened to your arguments, until the end of your debates.
32.12: I have followed you closely, and no one has convinced Job, none of you has refuted his words.
32.13: Do not say, "We have found wisdom; it is God who strikes him, not man."«
32.14: He did not direct his speeches against me, but I will not answer him with your words.
32.15: They are speechless, they don't answer, they are unable to speak.
32.16: I waited until they had finished speaking, until they remained silent and without answer.
32.17: Now it's my turn to speak; I also want to say what I think.
32.18: for I am full of talk, the spirit within me oppresses me.
32.19: My heart is like wine stored in a wineskin, like a wineskin full of new wine about to burst.
32.20: Let me speak, then, so that I may breathe easily, let my lips open to answer
32.21: I will not take sides with anyone, I will not flatter anyone
32.22: because I don't know how to flatter, otherwise my Creator would take me away immediately.

33.1: Now therefore, Job, listen to my words, pay attention to all my speeches.
33.2: Now I open my mouth, my tongue forms words on my palate,
33.3: My words will come from a righteous heart; pure truth will be expressed by my lips.
33.4: The spirit of God created me, the breath of the Almighty gives me life.
33.5: If you can, answer me, lay out your arguments before me, stand firm.
33.6: Before God I am your equal, like you I was formed from clay
33.7: Thus my fear will not frighten you and the weight of my majesty cannot overwhelm you.
33.8: Yes, you said it in my ears and I clearly heard the sound of your words,
33.9: «I am pure, free from all sin, I am blameless, there is no iniquity in me.”.
33.10: And God invents reasons for hatred against me; he treats me as his enemy.
33.11: He has shackled my feet, he watches my every step.»
33.12: I will tell you that in this you have not been just, for God is greater than man.
33.13: Why argue with him, because he doesn't answer to anyone for his actions?
33.14: Yet God speaks sometimes in one way, sometimes in another, and we pay no attention.
33.15: He speaks through dreams, through night visions, when a deep sleep weighs upon mortals, when they sleep in their beds.
33.16: At that moment, he opened men's ears and sealed his warnings there.,
33.17: in order to turn man away from his evil deeds and to remove pride from him,
33.18: in order to save his soul from death, his life from the sting of the dart.
33.19: Through pain, too, man is taken back to his bed, when a continuous struggle shakes his bones.
33.20: So he developed a disgust for bread and a horror of exquisite dishes.,
33.21: His flesh vanished before our eyes, his bones, which were previously unseen, were laid bare.
33.22: He approaches the pit, his life prey to the horrors of death.
33.23: But if he finds an angel among a thousand to intercede with him, who will make man aware of his duty,
33.24: God took pity on him and said to the angel, "Spare him from going down to the pit; I have found a ransom for his life."«
33.25: His flesh then has more freshness than in his early years; he returns to the days of his youth.
33.26: He prays to God and God is favorable to him, he contemplates His face with joy and the Most High restores his innocence.
33.27: He sings among men, he says: «I have sinned, I have violated justice, and God has not treated me according to my sins.
33.28: He spared my soul from descending into the pit, and my life flourishes in the light.»
33.29: There you have it, God does all this, twice, three times, for man.,
33.30: in order to bring him back from death, to illuminate him with the light of the living.
33.31: Pay attention, Job, listen to me, be silent, so that I may speak.
33.32: If you have something to say, answer me, speak, because I want to find you fair.
33.33: If you have nothing to say, listen to me, be silent and I will teach you wisdom.

34.2: Wise men, listen to my words; intelligent men, lend me your ear.
34.3: For the ear judges words as the palate discerns food.
34.4: Let us try to discern what is right, let us seek among ourselves what is good.
34.5: Job said: "I am innocent and God denies me justice.".
34.6: When I defend my right, I am considered a liar; my wound is painful, though I have not sinned.»
34.7: Is there a man like Job? He drinks blasphemy like water.
34.8: He associates with the artisans of iniquity, he walks with perverse men.
34.9: For he said, «It profits a man nothing to seek God’s favor.»
34.10: Listen to me, you wise men: Far be it from God to do wrong, far be it from the Almighty to do wrong.
34.11: He rewards man according to his deeds; he recompenses each person according to their ways.
34.12: No, indeed, God does not commit iniquity, the Almighty does not violate justice.
