34.13: Who gave him the government of the earth? Who entrusted him with the universe?
34.14: If he thought only of himself, if he withdrew his mind and his breath,
34.15: All flesh would instantly perish and man would return to dust.
34.16: If you have intelligence, listen to this, lend an ear to the sound of my words:
34.17: Would an enemy of justice hold supreme power? Do you dare condemn the Just, the Mighty?,
34.18: who calls a king "Scoundrel" and princes "Pervert"«
34.19: who does not take the side of the powerful, who does not look more on the rich than on the poor, because all are the work of his hands?
34.20: In an instant they perish, in the middle of the night, peoples stagger and disappear, the powerful are swept away without human intervention.
34.21: For the eyes of God are open to the ways of man; he sees clearly all his steps.
34.22: There is no darkness or shadow of death where those who commit iniquity can hide.
34.23: He doesn't need to look at a man twice to bring him to judgment with him.
34.24: He brings down the powerful without investigation and puts others in their place.
34.25: He therefore knows their works, he overthrows them at night and they are crushed.
34.26: He strikes them like impious people, in a place where they are being watched.,
34.27: because by turning away from him, by refusing to know all his ways,
34.28: They raised up to him the cry of the poor, they made him attentive to the cry of the unfortunate.
34.29: If he grants peace, who can find fault with him? If he hides his face, who can behold him, whether people or man, whom he treats thus?,
34.30: to put an end to the reign of the wicked, so that he will no longer be a snare for the people?
34.31: But had he said to God, "I have been punished, I will sin no more?",
34.32: "Show me what I do not know; if I have committed iniquity, shall I not do it again?"»
34.33: Is it according to your opinion that God should render justice so that you can reject his judgment? Choose as you please, not me; what you know, state it.
34.34: Sensible people will tell me, as will the wise man who is listening to me:
34.35: «Job spoke without understanding, and his speeches lacked wisdom.”.
34.36: Well, let Job be tested to the end, since his answers are those of an ungodly man.
34.37: For to the offense he adds rebellion, he claps his hands in our midst, he multiplies his words against God.»

35.2: Do you think it's just to say, "I'm right against God?"«
35.3: For you have said, «What good is my innocence to me? What do I gain compared to if I had sinned?»
35.4: I'm going to answer you and your friends at the same time.
35.5: Consider the heavens and look, see the clouds: they are higher than you.
35.6: If you sin, what harm do you cause him? If your offenses multiply, what do you do to him?
35.7: If you are just, what do you give him? What does he receive from your hand?
35.8: Your iniquity can only harm your fellow men, your righteousness is only useful to the son of man.
35.9: The unfortunate groan under the violence of the vexations and cry out under the hand of the powerful.
35.10: But no one says: "Where is God, my Creator, who gives songs of joy to the night?",
35.11: which has made us more intelligent than the animals of the earth, wiser than the birds of the air.»
35.12: They cry out then, without being heard, under the proud tyranny of the wicked.
35.13: God does not heed foolish speeches, the Almighty does not look at them.
35.14: When you tell him, "You don't see what's happening," your case is before him; await his judgment.
35.15: But, because his anger has not yet taken hold and he seems unaware of his madness,
35.16: Job lends his mouth to empty words and pours forth foolish speech.

36.2: Wait a little while and I will instruct you, for I still have words for the cause of God,
36.3: I will rise above my reasons and demonstrate the justice of my Creator.
36.4: Rest assured, my words are free from falsehood; before you stands a man sincere in his judgments.
36.5: Behold, God is mighty, yet he does not despise anyone; he is mighty in the strength of his understanding.
36.6: He does not let the wicked live and he brings justice to the unfortunate.
36.7: He does not turn his eyes away from the righteous, he seats them on the throne with kings, he establishes them forever, and they are exalted.
36.8: If they fall into chains, if they are caught in the bonds of misfortune,
36.9: He denounces their works, their faults caused by pride.
36.10: He opens their ears to rebuke, he urges them to turn away from evil.
36.11: If they listen and submit, they will end their days in happiness and their years in delight.
36.12: But if they do not listen, they will perish by the sword; they will die in their blindness.
36.13: The ungodly hearts give themselves over to anger; they do not cry out to God when he puts them in chains.
36.14: Thus they die in their youth and their lives wither like those of the infamous.
36.15: But God saves the unfortunate in their misery; he teaches them through suffering.
36.16: He will also bring you out of distress and set you free, and your table will be prepared with rich food.
36.17: But if you fill the measure of the wicked, you will bear the sentence and the punishment.
36.18: Beware that God, in his anger, may inflict punishment upon you, and that your rich offerings may lead you astray.
36.19: Will your cries pull you out of distress and even summon all your strength?
36.20: Do not sigh for the night, during which peoples are annihilated on the spot.
36.21: Beware of turning to iniquity, for you prefer it to affliction.
36.22: Look: God is sublime in his power. What master is like him?
36.23: Who shows him the path he should follow? Who can tell him, "You did wrong?"«
36.24: Think instead of glorifying his works, which men celebrate in their songs.
36.25: Every man admires them, mortals contemplate them from afar.
36.26: God is great beyond all knowledge; the number of his years is unfathomable.
36.27: It attracts the drops of water, which spread out as rain under their weight.
36.28: The clouds let it flow and fall upon the mass of men.
36.29: Who can comprehend the expansion of the clouds and the roaring of the tent of the Most High?
36.30: Sometimes it spreads its light around itself, sometimes it hides itself as if at the bottom of the sea.
36.31: This is how he exercises his justice over the peoples and provides food in abundance.
36.32: He takes the light in his hands and marks the goal to be reached.
36.33: Its thunder announces it, the terror of the flocks announces its approach.

37.1: At this sight, my heart is trembling all over, it leaps out of its place.
37.2: Listen, listen to the crash of his voice, the growl that comes out of his mouth.
37.3: He gives it free rein under the vastness of the heavens, and his lightning shines to the ends of the earth.
37.4: Then a roar erupts, he thunders with his majestic voice, he no longer holds back the lightning, when his voice is heard,
37.5: God thunders with his voice in a wonderful way. He does great things that we do not understand.
37.6: He said to the snow, "Fall on the earth," he commanded the showers and torrential rains.
37.7: He places a seal on the hand of every man, so that every mortal may recognize his Creator.
37.8: Then the wild animal returns to its lair and remains in its den.
37.9: The hurricane emerges from its hidden retreats, the north wind brings the frost.
37.10: At God's breath ice forms and the mass of water is imprisoned.
37.11: It fills the clouds with vapors, it disperses its luminous clouds.
37.12: According to his decrees, we see them wandering in all directions, to carry out all that he commands them, on the face of the inhabited earth.
37.13: He sends them sometimes as punishment for his land and sometimes as a sign of favor.
37.14: Job, pay attention to these things, stop and consider the wonders of God.
37.15: Do you know how he operates them and how the clouds cause lightning to strike?
37.16: Do you understand the swaying of the clouds, the wonders of him whose science is perfect?,
37.17: you whose clothes are warm, when the earth rests in the midday breeze?
37.18: Can you, like him, stretch out the clouds and make them as solid as a bronze mirror?
37.19: Tell us what we should say to him: we cannot speak to him, being ignorant as we are.
37.20: Ah, don't let anyone repeat my speeches to him. Has any man ever said he wished for his own downfall?
37.21: We can no longer see the sunlight shining behind the clouds, but when a wind passes, it disperses them.
37.22: The gold comes from the north, but God, how awesome is his majesty.
37.23: The Almighty, we cannot reach him: he is great in power and in right and in justice, he answers to no one.
37.24: Let men therefore revere him. He does not look upon those who consider themselves wise.

38.1: Then the Lord answered Job out of the midst of the storm and said:
38.2: Who is it that obscures the divine plan in this way, with words devoid of understanding?
38.3: Gird up your loins like a man: I am going to question you and you shall answer me.
38.4: Where were you when I laid the foundations of the earth? Tell me, if you have understanding.
38.5: Who determined its dimensions? Do you know? Who stretched the line across it?
38.6: On what do its foundations rest, or who laid its cornerstone?,
38.7: when the morning stars sang in chorus and all the sons of God shouted for joy?
38.8: Who shut the sea with gates, when it burst forth impetuous from its mother's womb?,
38.9: when I gave him clouds for clothes and thick mists for swaddling clothes,
38.10: when I imposed my law upon him, when I put doors and bolts in his way,
38.11: and that I say to him: "You will come this far, not beyond, here the pride of your waves will stop"?
38.12: Have you, since you came into existence, given orders in the morning? Have you assigned its place at dawn?,
38.13: so that it may grasp the ends of the earth and shake off its wicked,
38.14: so that the earth may take shape, like clay under the seal, and appear adorned as with a garment,
38.15: So that wrongdoers may be deprived of their light and the arm raised for crime may be broken?
38.16: Have you gone down to the springs of the sea, have you walked in the depths of the abyss?
38.17: Have the gates of death been opened before you? Have you seen the gates of the dark abode?
38.18: Have you embraced the expanse of the earth? Speak, if you know all these things.
38.19: Where is the path that leads to the abode of light, and where is the dwelling place of darkness?
38.20: You could seize them in their domain; you know the paths of their stay.
38.21: You probably know this, since you were born before them, the number of your days is so great.
38.22: Have you entered the treasures of the snow? Have you seen the reservoirs of the hail?,
38.23: that I have ready for the time of distress, for the days of war and combat?
38.24: By what means does the light divide and the east wind spread over the earth?
38.25: Who opened channels for the downpours and traced a path for the fires of thunder,
38.26: so that rain may fall on an uninhabited land, on the desert where there are no people,
38.27: so that it may water the vast, empty plain and make the green grass sprout there.
38.28: Does rain have a father? Who gives birth to the dewdrops?
38.29: From whose breast does ice spring? And the frost of the sky, which gives birth to it,
38.30: so that the waters may harden like stone and the surface of the abyss may solidify?
38.31: Is it you who tightens the bonds of the Pleiades, or could you loosen the chains of Orion?
38.32: Is it you who makes the constellations rise in their time, who guides the Bear with her cubs?
38.33: Do you know the laws of heaven, do you regulate its influences on earth?
38.34: Do you raise your voice to the heavens, so that torrents of water fall upon you?
38.35: Is it you who releases the lightning bolts so that they leave and tell you, "Here we are"?«
38.36: Who placed wisdom in the clouds, or who gave intelligence to the meteors?
38.37: Who can accurately count the clouds, tilt the wineskins of the sky,
38.38: so that the dust forms into a solid mass and the clods stick together?
38.39: Is it you who hunts for the lioness's prey, who satisfies the hunger of the lion cubs?,
38.40: When they are lying in their den, lying in ambush in the thicket?
38.41: Who prepares food for the crow, when its young cry out to God, wandering here and there without food?

39.1: Do you know the time when wild goats have their babies? Have you ever watched does giving birth?
39.2: Have you counted the months of their litter and do you know the time of their delivery?
39.3: They kneel, lay down their little ones, and are rid of their pain.
39.4: Their fawns grow strong and mature in the fields, then they leave and never return.
39.5: Who set the onager free, who broke the wild donkey's bonds?,
39.6: To whom have I given the desert as a home, the salt plain as a dwelling place?
39.7: He despises the tumult of cities, he does not hear the cries of a master.
39.8: He roams the mountains in search of his pasture, following the slightest traces of greenery.
39.9: Will the buffalo want to serve you, or will it spend the night in its stable?
39.10: Will you tie it with a rope to the furrow, or will it harrow behind you in the valleys?
39.11: Will you trust him because he is very strong? Will you let him do your work?
39.12: Will you rely on him to bring in your harvest, to gather the wheat on your threshing floor?
39.13: The ostrich's wing beats joyfully; it has neither the down nor the plumage of the stork.
39.14: She abandons her eggs on the earth and leaves them to heat up on the sand.
39.15: She forgets that feet can trample them, that beasts of the field can crush them.
39.16: She is harsh with her children, as if they were not her own; she does not worry that her work is in vain.
39.17: For God denied him wisdom and did not bestow understanding upon him.
39.18: But when she beats her flanks and takes flight, she laughs at the horse and rider.
39.19: Is it you who gives the horse its strength, who clothes its neck with a flowing mane?,
39.20: What makes him leap like a grasshopper? His proud neighing spreads terror.
39.21: He digs his feet into the earth, he is proud of his strength, he rushes forward into battle.
39.22: He laughs at fear, nothing frightens him, he does not back down from the sword.
39.23: The quiver, the gleaming lance, and the javelin resounded above him.
39.24: It trembles, it stirs, it devours the ground, it can no longer contain itself when the trumpet sounds.
39.25: At the sound of the trumpet, he said, "Let's go." From afar he could smell the battle, the thundering voice of the leaders and the cries of the warriors.
39.26: Is it through your wisdom that the sparrowhawk takes flight and spreads its wings towards the south?
39.27: Is it at your command that the eagle rises and builds its nest on the heights?
39.28: He dwells in the rocks, he makes his home in the teeth of the stone, on the summits.
39.29: From there, he watches for his prey, his gaze piercing into the distance.
39.30: Its young feed on blood; wherever there are corpses, it can be found.

40.1: The Lord, speaking to Job, said:
40.2: Does the Almighty's censor still wish to plead against him? Can he who argues with God answer?
40.4: Wretched as I am, what can I answer you? I put my hand over my mouth.
40.5: I have spoken once, I will not reply; twice, I will add nothing.
40.6: The Lord spoke again to Job from the midst of the storm and said:
40.7: Gird up your loins, like a man, I am going to question you and you shall answer me.
40.8: Do you want to destroy my justice, to condemn me in order to have rights?
40.9: Do you have an arm like God's and do you thunder with his voice?
40.10: Adorn yourself with grandeur and magnificence, clothe yourself with glory and majesty,
40.11: unleash the floods of your anger, with a look that humbles all pride.
40.12: With a single glance, he can make everything magnificent bend, crushing the wicked on the spot.,
40.13: Hide them all together in the dust, enclose their faces in darkness.
40.14: So, I too will pay you homage, that your right hand can save you.
40.15: Look at Behemoth, whom I created like you: he feeds on grass, like an ox.
40.16: Look, his strength is in his loins and his power in the muscles of his sides.
40.17: It raises its tail like a cedar, the nerves in its thighs form a solid bundle.
40.18: His bones are like bronze tubes, his ribs like iron bars.
40.19: This is God's masterpiece; its Creator has equipped it with a sword.
40.20: The mountains provide fodder for him, and around him all the beasts of the fields play.
40.21: He lies down beneath the lotuses, in the secrecy of the reeds and marshes.
40.22: Lotuses cover it with their shade, willows from the stream surround it.
40.23: He does not fear that the river should overflow; he would be calm if the Jordan rose to his mouth.
40.24: Is it from the front that we can seize him, with nets, and pierce his nostrils?
40.25: Will you pull Leviathan out with a hook and strangle his tongue with a rope?
40.26: Will you put a ring through his nostrils and pierce his jaw with a ring?
40.27: Will he offer you fervent prayers, will he speak sweet words to you?
40.28: Will he make an alliance with you? Will you always take him into your service?
40.29: Will you play with him like a sparrow, will you tie him up to amuse your daughters?
40.30: Do the fishermen involved trade it, or do they share it among the merchants?
40.31: Will you riddle his skin with darts, will you pierce his head with a harpoon?
40.32: Try to get your hands on him: remember the fight and you won't go back to it.

41.1: The hunter's expectations are thus thwarted; the sight of the monster is enough to overwhelm him.
41.2: No one is bold enough to provoke Leviathan: who would dare to resist me face to face?
41.3: Who has compelled me, that I should have to repay him? All that is under heaven is mine.
41.4: I do not want to remain silent about its members, its strength, the harmony of its structure.
41.5: Who has ever lifted the edge of their breastplate? Who has crossed the double line of their rack?
41.6: Who opened the doors of its mouth? Terror dwells around its teeth.
41.7: The lines of its scales are superb, like tightly fitting seals.
41.8: Each one touches its neighbor; not a breath would pass between them.
41.9: They adhere to one another, they are joined and cannot be separated.
41.10: Her sneezes bring forth light, her eyes are like the eyelids of dawn.
41.11: Flames erupt from its mouth, sparks of fire fly out.
41.12: Smoke comes out of his nostrils, as if from a burning, boiling cauldron.
41.13: His breath ignites the coals, the flame leaps from his mouth.
41.14: Strength resides in his neck, terror leaps before him.
41.15: The muscles of his flesh held together, fused to him, unshakeable.
41.16: His heart is as hard as stone, as hard as the lower millstone.
41.17: When he rises, even the bravest are afraid; terror makes them faint.
41.18: Attack him with the sword, and the sword cannot withstand him, nor the spear, nor the javelin, nor the arrow.
41.19: He considers iron to be straw, and bronze to be rotten wood.
41.20: The girl with the bow does not frighten him away; the stones of the sling are but straw to him.,
41.21: the club, a wisp of straw, he laughs at the crash of the pikes.
41.22: Beneath its belly are sharp shards: it looks like a harrow that it is spreading over the silt.
41.23: He makes the deep boil like a cauldron, he makes the sea a vessel of perfumes.
41.24: He leaves behind a trail of light; it's as if the abyss has white hair.
41.25: He has no equal on earth; he was created to fear nothing.
41.26: He looks straight at everything that is raised; he is the king of the proudest animals.

42.1: Job answered the Lord and said:
42.2: I know you can do anything and that no goal is too difficult for you.
42.3: «"Who is it that obscures the divine plan, without knowing?" Yes, I have spoken unintelligently of wonders that are beyond me and that I do not know.
42.4: «"Listen to me, I'm going to speak, I'm going to question you, answer me."»
42.5: My ear had heard of you, but now my eye has seen you.
42.6: That is why I condemn myself and repent, in dust and ashes.
42.7: After the Lord had spoken these words to Job, he said to Eliphaz the Temanite, «My anger is kindled against you and against your two friends, because you have not spoken of me according to the truth, as my servant Job has.
42.8: Now take seven young bulls and seven rams, and come to my servant Job and offer a burnt offering for yourselves. My servant Job will pray for you, and I will not deal with you according to your folly, because you have not spoken of me truthfully, as my servant Job has.»
42.9: Eliphaz the Temanite, Baldad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naaman went and did as the Lord had told them, and the Lord accepted Job’s prayer.
42.10: The Lord restored Job to his former state, while Job interceded for his friends, and the Lord restored to Job twice all his possessions.
42.11: His brothers, sisters, and old friends all came to visit him and ate with him in his house. They pitied him and comforted him for all the misfortunes the Lord had brought upon him, and each gave him a silver coin and a gold ring.
42.12: And the Lord blessed the latter days of Job more than the former, and he had fourteen thousand sheep, six thousand camels, a thousand yoke of oxen, and a thousand donkeys.
42.13: He had seven sons and three daughters.,
42.14: He named the first Jemima, the second Ketsia, and the third Keren-Hapouk.
42.15: Nowhere in all the earth were there women as beautiful as Job's daughters, and their father gave them a share of the inheritance among their brothers.
42.140: years, and he saw his sons and his sons' sons to the fourth generation.
42.17: And Job died old and full of days.
